title: Erdogan Returns from Kazakhstan With Energy Deals, Cabinet to Tackle Hormuz Fallout and PKK Process
subhead: Turkey's cabinet meets Monday to address the economic strain of the Hormuz crisis and the next phase of the PKK peace process, as Erdogan wraps up a Central Asia visit that locked in Kazakh oil transit and drone production deals. Meanwhile, the central bank's inflation forecast doubled, Istanbul's jailed mayor denounced his trial, and FIFA-Iran talks in Istanbul signaled Turkey's quiet diplomatic role.

sections.heading: Erdogan's Kazakhstan Tour Locks In Energy and Defense Deals
sections.body:
President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan returned from Kazakhstan on Friday after signing 13 bilateral agreements and a Declaration on Eternal Friendship and Expanded Strategic Partnership with President Kassym-Jomart Tokayev. The most consequential deal: Kazakh oil transit through the Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan pipeline, giving Ankara a new lever as Gulf routes remain disrupted by the Iran war.

Erdogan told reporters the two countries aim to raise bilateral trade from $10 billion to $15 billion, with nearly 5,500 Turkish companies already invested $6 billion in Kazakhstan. Turkish Petroleum (TPAO) signed separate memorandums with KazMunayGas on oil field development and joint oil and gas projects.

Beyond energy, the visit produced a deal to jointly produce Baykar's Anka unmanned aerial vehicles in Kazakhstan and a TAV Holding agreement to expand Almaty International Airport. Erdoğan also received the newly created Order of Khoja Ahmed Yasawi — the first ever awarded.

At the informal Organization of Turkic States summit in Turkistan, leaders signed the Turkistan Declaration, pledging to deepen digital and AI cooperation and strengthen institutional ties among Turkic states. Erdoğan said this year's OTS summit will be held in Turkey, and also confirmed the upcoming NATO summit in Ankara in July.
sections.whyItMatters: The Kazakh oil deal directly responds to the Hormuz disruption, positioning the BTC pipeline as a viable alternative route for Central Asian crude. The Anka drone co-production agreement extends Turkey's defense export footprint deeper into Central Asia, a region where both China and Russia have traditionally dominated.

sections.heading: Cabinet to Address Hormuz Fallout and Energy Diversification
sections.body:
Erdoğan will chair a cabinet meeting on Monday — his first since returning from Kazakhstan — with the Hormuz crisis impact on the Turkish economy and the terror-free Türkiye initiative topping the agenda.

Ministers will discuss the Strait of Hormuz deadlock and its consequences for energy prices and supply. Iran's parliament national security committee head Ebrahim Azizi said Saturday that Iran will soon announce a new mechanism to regulate shipping through Hormuz, including designated routes and service fees. Only commercial vessels from countries that cooperate with Iran will be allowed passage, Azizi said — calling the U.S. naval escort operation a 'freedom project' that would be excluded.

The backdrop is stark: Iraq's oil exports through Hormuz plunged from 93 million barrels per month to just 10 million in April, according to Iraq's new oil minister. Iran has collected its first revenues from tolls imposed on the waterway and insists maritime traffic will 'not return to its pre-war status.'

Iran's conditions for a broader deal — reported by Fars news on Sunday — include recognition of Iranian sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz, ending the war on all fronts including Lebanon, lifting sanctions, releasing frozen assets, and compensation for war damage. The U.S. reportedly counters with demands including allowing only one Iranian nuclear facility to remain operational and transferring 400 kg of Iranian uranium to the U.S.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey imports significant energy through routes affected by the Hormuz standoff. The cabinet meeting signals Ankara is treating the crisis as a sustained economic threat, not a temporary disruption — and the Kazakh oil deal is part of that response.

sections.heading: Central Bank Doubles Inflation Forecast as Energy Prices Bite
sections.body:
The Central Bank of the Republic of Türkiye raised its year-end inflation forecast for 2026 to 26%, up from 18%, Governor Fatih Karahan announced Thursday. The bank also revised its interim target upward to 24% from 16%.

Annual inflation rose to 32.4% in April, with energy inflation surging 19 percentage points to 47% over the past two months alone. Electricity and natural gas tariffs were revised upward as import costs climbed. The bank abandoned its forecast range format, citing 'unexpectedly severe' uncertainty from the Iran war and energy disruptions.

The policy rate remains at 37% after a single 100-basis-point cut in January. Authorities suspended one-week repo auctions in March and pushed the effective reference rate to around 40% to tighten conditions. The bank projects inflation declining to 15% by end-2027 and 9% by end-2028.

April trade data showed exports rising while imports declined, narrowing the trade deficit — but the bank warned that continued geopolitical instability and high oil prices could increase pressure on the current account for the rest of the year.
sections.whyItMatters: The forecast revision is the clearest quantitative signal yet of how the Hormuz standoff is hitting the Turkish economy. With energy inflation at 47% and the central bank effectively re-tightening monetary conditions, the war's economic ripple effects are no longer projections — they're showing up in consumer prices.

sections.heading: PKK Peace Process: Bahçeli Proposes 'Peace Coordinator' and Status for Öcalan
sections.body:
MHP leader Devlet Bahçeli proposed creating a 'Peace Process and Politicization Coordinatorship' — a formal body to oversee the next phase of the terror-free Türkiye initiative — and argued that Abdullah Öcalan's legal status must be addressed for the process to work.

'The issue of Abdullah Öcalan's status is important to us. The process can't work in a healthy manner by acting as though this issue does not exist,' Bahçeli said at an MHP parliamentary group meeting on May 5. He framed the mechanism as serving state interests: safeguarding public order, national security, and 'democratic participation.'

Monday's cabinet meeting will discuss the latest stage of the terror-free initiative and legal steps to speed it up. Media reports say security forces are completing a report on monitoring PKK disarmament. If confirmed, parliament could begin work on legal amendments including lenient sentences for surrendering PKK members and early release for convicted members under certain conditions.

Erdogan said Saturday that Türkiye aims to divert counterterrorism spending to education, science, employment, transportation, and technology, calling the initiative's ultimate goal the safety of future generations.
sections.whyItMatters: Bahçeli's proposal is the most specific institutional design yet for the post-PKK transition. The question of Öcalan's status is politically explosive — any move perceived as granting legitimacy to a convicted terrorist leader would face fierce nationalist opposition, but Bahçeli's own nationalist credentials give him cover to push the idea.

sections.heading: İmamoğlu Denounces 'Manufactured' Trial as Corruption Probe Expands
sections.body:
Jailed Istanbul Mayor Ekrem İmamoğlu condemned 'the autocracy that manufactured this unfair trial' during a court appearance at Silivri prison complex, according to a detailed profile published Sunday by Le Monde. Facing 142 charges of corruption, espionage, and defamation across an 80,000-page indictment, İmamoğlu — the declared opposition candidate for the 2028 presidential election — appeared 'worn down' after more than 13 months in detention, the report said.

The trial continues against İmamoğlu and 413 co-defendants, including other Istanbul elected officials and city employees. The case could drag on for months or even years.

Meanwhile, Istanbul's expanding corruption investigation led to the detention of 12 suspects on Friday, according to Daily Sabah. The probe targets the opposition-run Istanbul municipality and is linked to the broader case against İmamoğlu.
sections.whyItMatters: The İmamoğlu trial remains the most visible symbol of Turkey's democratic backsliding for Western observers, and the expanding corruption probe signals that pressure on the opposition is intensifying rather than easing. With 413 co-defendants and potential sentencing of up to 2,352 years, the case's scale is itself a political statement.

sections.heading: FIFA-Iran Talks in Istanbul Highlight Turkey's Quiet Diplomatic Role
sections.body:
FIFA Secretary-General Mattias Grafstrom held what he called an 'excellent and constructive' meeting with Iranian Football Federation president Mehdi Taj in Istanbul on Saturday, addressing Iran's participation in the 2026 World Cup despite the ongoing U.S.-Iran conflict.

Iran raised 10 points of concern, and FIFA offered solutions for each, Taj said. The Iranian squad is scheduled to travel to a training camp in Turkey on Monday, before proceeding to its base in Tucson, Arizona, for the tournament starting June 15.

Iran had requested its group-stage matches be moved from the United States to Mexico — a request FIFA president Gianni Infantino denied. Iranian FA president Taj, who has IRGC ties that previously barred him from the FIFA Congress in Vancouver, said he hoped the team could compete 'without any problems.'

Istanbul's role as a neutral venue for the FIFA-Iran talks underscores Turkey's continued function as a diplomatic intermediary — even as it hosts NATO's upcoming July summit.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is simultaneously a NATO member hosting the alliance's July summit and a neutral venue for FIFA-Iran negotiations involving a country at war with the U.S. That duality is the defining feature of Ankara's foreign policy: maintaining channels with all sides.

sections.heading: Erdogan's Demographic Push Meets Economic Reality
sections.body:
Erdoğan unveiled Turkey's 2026-2035 Family and Population 10-Year Vision, designating the final week of May as 'National Family Week.' The plan sets five priorities: protecting the family institution, encouraging marriage, increasing the fertility rate, improving youth development and elderly welfare, and supporting rural development.

Turkey's fertility rate fell to 1.48 in 2024 — well below the 2.1 replacement level, where it has been since 2017. Annual births dropped from 1.35 million in 2014 to below 1 million in 2023. Erdoğan called the figures 'worrying for all of us' and framed demographics as 'a matter of national survival.'

Concrete measures include raising marriage loan support from 150,000 liras to 200,000-250,000 liras, extending maternity leave to 24 weeks, priority social housing for families with three or more children, and higher birth support payments.

Yet the timing is awkward: with energy inflation at 47% and overall inflation at 32.4%, the economic pressures that deter families from having children are intensifying, not easing. Population Matters noted that Turkey's fertility decline mirrors global patterns in middle-income countries and that restrictive policies on contraception have not reversed the trend — they have only limited women's autonomy.
sections.whyItMatters: The demographic push collides directly with the economic fallout of the Iran war. Higher energy bills and 32% inflation make the government's family incentives look thin against the cost of raising a child — estimated at 15-20 times annual household income for an average family.

bottomLine: Erdogan returns from Kazakhstan with energy and defense deals that respond directly to the Hormuz crisis, but the economic damage is already showing: inflation at 32.4%, energy prices surging 47%, and the central bank forced to nearly double its forecast. Monday's cabinet meeting will tackle both the Hormuz fallout and the next phase of the PKK peace process, where Bahçeli's push for a formal 'peace coordinator' and legal status for Öcalan marks the most ambitious — and most politically risky — institutional design yet. Meanwhile, İmamoğlu's trial grinds on, FIFA uses Istanbul as neutral ground for Iran talks, and the government's demographic push runs headlong into the inflation it's struggling to contain.
sources: Daily Sabah, Le Monde, Astana Times, 1news.az, Turkey Today, Anadolu Agency, Reuters, France24, Sportstar/The Hindu, Population Matters, Hürriyet Daily News, Trading Economics, Fars News, MSN, WSJ, CBC News
